Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. Being 29 with limited resources means I'm juggling a lot outside of school, so studying happened whenever I could squeeze it in. Some weeks I'd manage to sit down with my notes for a few hours, other weeks life just got in the way. I tried to aim for about two hours a day but that rarely happened consistently. Most of my studying was reactive rather than planned.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. I'd go days without touching anything academic, then suddenly realize a deadline was coming and try to cram everything in. Work and other responsibilities always seemed to take priority, and by the time I had energy for studying, I was already behind. It wasn't intentional neglect, just the reality of my situation.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or assignment that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get an email reminder and think, 'Oh right, I should check what's happening in there.' It wasn't a habit I enjoyed or looked forward to. The platform felt like another thing demanding my attention when I was already stretched thin.
Q4: Not really, no. There were brief periods where I'd log in a few days in a row, but it never stuck. The gamified tasks they introduced were mildly interesting at first, but my motivation is just so low these days that even earning points or badges didn't make me want to come back regularly. Life outside the course always pulled me away.
Q5: The reading materials were most useful for me since that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs and go through them at my own pace, highlighting and making notes. The videos were okay too, but honestly I preferred having text I could read and re-read. Discussion forums never really clicked for me even though I posted a couple of times.
Q6: I ignored most of the interactive activities and discussion threads. They felt like extra work that wouldn't directly help my grades, and I was already struggling to complete the basics. The gamified elements they added seemed designed for more engaged students, not someone like me who's just trying to survive. I didn't have the mental energy for extras.
Q7: If it directly contributed to an assignment or exam, it felt worth my time. I needed to see a clear connection between the effort I was putting in and my final grade. Anything that seemed optional or supplementary just fell off my radar. With limited time and energy, I had to be strategic about where I invested myself.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones at least had consequences attached, so I'd force myself through them even when I didn't want to. Optional stuff just made me feel guilty because I knew I probably should do it but couldn't muster the motivation. The gamified tasks fell somewhere in between but still felt optional enough to skip.
Q9: Yes, a lot of my actual learning happened offline. I'd download the reading materials and work through them on my phone during commutes or breaks at work. I have notebooks full of handwritten notes that the LMS would never know about. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos or free resources online that explained concepts better than the course materials.
Q10: There were many times I was genuinely working through concepts in my head or reviewing my handwritten notes, but I wasn't logged into anything. I'd spend hours reading downloaded PDFs without the system registering any activity. My exam score reflects understanding I built through methods that were completely invisible to the platform.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF or save a video to watch later, and then I'd never need to go back to the LMS for that material. It was more convenient for me to have things saved locally since my internet isn't always reliable. The system probably thought I only looked at something once when really I studied it multiple times offline.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. I was feeling quite sad and overwhelmed, and the stress was affecting my sleep and concentration. Financial pressures made it hard to focus on academics when I was worried about more immediate concerns. Those weeks I barely logged in at all.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or dealing with personal stuff, the LMS was the last thing on my mind. Confusion about assignments made me avoid them rather than seek help, which I know wasn't smart. The competing responsibilities of being an adult student with limited resources meant the course often took a backseat to survival.
Q14: Many times I'd open materials and just stare at them without anything sinking in. My mind would be elsewhere, worrying about bills or feeling that persistent sadness that's been hanging over me. I'd scroll through pages or let videos play but I wasn't really present. Those sessions felt like going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety of starting something I wasn't confident about made avoidance feel easier in the moment. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, then tomorrow would come with its own problems. My assignment completion rate shows how much I struggled with this.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd ignore everything until the last possible moment, then rush to submit whatever I could manage. It wasn't a healthy approach but it was the only way I functioned. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged with the LMS at all.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss for me. Sometimes getting a decent grade would give me a small boost, but negative feedback just confirmed my fears that I wasn't cut out for this. I didn't always check feedback promptly because I was afraid of what it might say. It rarely motivated me to engage more.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming at times, with too many components to keep track of. I appreciated having reading materials I could access anytime, but the multiple assignment types and discussion requirements were hard to manage. A simpler structure might have helped me stay more organized and less anxious about missing things.
Q19: The gamified elements were supposed to make engagement easier, but for someone with low motivation like me, they felt like another thing to fail at. What made it harder was not having clear priorities about what mattered most. What helped was having downloadable materials since that worked with my limited internet access.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for understanding but struggling to produce work. They could be reading everything, watching all the videos, trying to learn, but when it comes to actually writing assignments, something blocks them. Anxiety, perfectionism, confusion about expectations, or just being overwhelmed by life outside school.
Q21: That's basically my story. My exam score was decent because I understood the material through my own methods, but my LMS activity was minimal. I learned through downloaded readings, offline study, and my own notes. The platform only captured a fraction of my actual engagement with the course content.
Q22: Course logs can show when someone logs in and clicks on things, but they can't show understanding or effort. They miss all the offline studying, the mental processing, the times you're genuinely trying but struggling. They probably overvalue clicking around and undervalue deep, focused learning that happens away from screens.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and making genuine effort to learn, even when it's hard. It's not just about logging in or completing tasks mechanically. For me, engagement happened in quiet moments with my notes, not necessarily when I was active on the platform. It's internal as much as external.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my emotional state, the sadness I've been carrying, or how hard I've been fighting just to keep going. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with downloaded materials or the financial stress that pulled my attention away. It definitely wouldn't show that despite my low activity, I actually learned quite a bit and earned a Merit grade.
